title: Speak your mother tongue. Pass it on.
norm: Use your mother tongue at home and teach it to your children - alongside the link languages they'll need for school and work - instead of letting it fall silent.
wit: A language doesn't die loudly. It just stops being spoken to children.
affects: every Indian who shares this heritage
goesRight: A language lives only while it is spoken and taught. Pass yours on, and the songs, stories, and knowledge carried in it live on too.

impact.cause: Each generation actually speaking and teaching its mother tongue
impact.mechanism: Living heritage survives only as long as it is passed person-to-person; a language not spoken to children is not passed on, and is lost with its last speakers
impact.effect: The language - and the knowledge and identity it carries - reaches the next generation instead of ending
impact.supported_by: The importance of intangible cultural heritage is not the cultural manifestation itself but rather the wealth of knowledge and skills that is transmitted through it from one generation to the next.
impact.caveat: UNESCO's general definition of how living heritage passes on, applied here to language, which UNESCO lists as a vehicle of intangible heritage.

closer: You couldn't have known. Now you do.

title: Take photos. Leave the wall alone.
norm: Never carve, paint, scratch, stick anything on, or chip a piece off a heritage monument. The damage is permanent - and it's a crime.
wit: 'Was here' outlives you on that wall, and not in a good way.
affects: the shared record of the past
goesRight: Stone and plaster don't heal. Treat a monument as untouchable and it survives whole for the next generation - and you stay on the right side of the law.

impact.cause: Carving, painting, scratching, or chipping pieces off a protected monument
impact.mechanism: Damage to historic stone and plaster can't be undone; Indian law treats harming a nationally protected monument as a punishable offence for exactly that reason
impact.effect: Permanent, unrepairable loss of the monument - and criminal liability for whoever does it

magnitude.basis: AMASR Act 1958, s.30: up to 2 years' imprisonment, or a fine up to Rs 1,00,000, or both

impact.source_title: Section 30, The Ancient Monuments and Archaeological Sites and Remains Act, 1958
impact.publisher: Government of India (AMASR Act 1958), via Indian Kanoon
impact.supported_by: destroys, removes, injures, alters, defaces, imperils or misuse a protected monument ... imprisonment which may extend to two years ... fine which may extend to one lakh rupees ... or both
impact.caveat: Applies to monuments of national importance under the AMASR Act (fine raised to one lakh rupees in 2010); state-protected and unprotected sites fall under separate laws.

closer: You couldn't have known. Now you do.

title: Learn the old skill. Teach it on.
norm: Learn a craft, song, recipe, or story from those who still hold it - and teach it to someone younger - so the know-how isn't lost when they're gone.
wit: A photo of a craft isn't the craft. Only a pair of taught hands is.
goesRight: A craft lives only in the hands and memory of people who practise it. Learn it from an elder and teach it onward, and it survives - even where a photo would not have saved it.

impact.cause: A younger person learning a traditional skill directly from those who hold it
impact.mechanism: Traditional crafts pass by hands-on teaching, often within families, not from books; if no one learns from today's holders, the technique goes when they do
impact.effect: The skill and its knowledge continue instead of dying out
impact.source_title: Traditional brass and copper craft of utensil making among the Thatheras of Jandiala Guru, Punjab, India
impact.publisher: UNESCO (Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity)
impact.supported_by: The process of manufacturing is transmitted orally from father to son.
impact.caveat: One inscribed example of hand-to-hand transmission, used neutrally - not to privilege any region or community.

impact.cause: Communities keeping living traditions in active practice
impact.mechanism: Intangible heritage is 'living' - it survives only by being practised and handed down
impact.effect: A sense of identity and continuity carried across generations
impact.supported_by: traditions or living expressions inherited from our ancestors and passed on to our descendants

closer: You couldn't have known. Now you do.
